This calculator shows you exactly how much each meeting costs based on the number of attendees, duration, and their average salary. Meeting costs add up quickly—on average, meetings consume about 30% of employee work time. Use this tool to identify costly meetings and make data-driven decisions about scheduling and participation.

explanation:
## Understanding Meeting Costs

Meetings are one of the largest hidden costs in business operations. Research shows that meetings consume approximately 30% of employee work time, yet many organizations don't track or analyze meeting expenses.

## How to Calculate Meeting Cost

The formula is straightforward:

**Meeting Cost = (Monthly Salary ÷ 176 hours) × Duration (hours) × Number of Attendees**

Where 176 hours is the standard full-time work month (220 work days ÷ 5 days per week × 8 hours per day ≈ 176 hours).

## Benchmarks for Meeting Costs

Typical meeting costs vary by organization size and employee salary levels:

| Meeting Type | Duration | Typical Attendees | Estimated Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quick Standup | 15 min | 5-8 | $50–$200 |
| Status Update | 30 min | 8-10 | $200–$600 |
| Planning Session | 1 hour | 10-12 | $500–$1,500 |
| All-Hands | 1 hour | 50+ | $2,500+ |

## How to Reduce Meeting Costs

- **Set a clear agenda** and stick to it—aimless meetings are the most expensive.
- **Limit duration**: Many meetings could be cut to 25 or 50 minutes instead of a full hour.
- **Required attendance only**: Not everyone needs to be in every meeting.
- **Use async communication**: Slack, email, or shared documents for non-urgent updates.
- **Timeboxing**: Allocate specific time for each agenda item.
- **Daily standups**: 15 minutes daily often delivers more value than a 1-hour weekly meeting.
- **Monthly retros**: Schedule recurring meetings and stick to them—ad hoc meetings are less efficient.

## Important Notes

This calculator provides an estimate for reference purposes. Actual meeting costs may vary based on your organization's salary structure, benefits, and overhead allocation. For detailed cost analysis, consult with your finance or HR department.

faq.a: Multiply the hourly salary (monthly salary ÷ 176 hours) by the meeting duration in hours, then multiply by the number of attendees.

faq.q: What is the standard hourly cost from monthly salary?
faq.a: Divide monthly salary by 176 hours, which represents a standard full-time work month.

faq.q: Why do meeting costs seem so high?
faq.a: Multiple salaries are being spent simultaneously. An 8-person meeting for 1 hour costs what one person earns in 8 hours.

faq.q: How can I reduce meeting costs?
faq.a: Set clear agendas, limit attendees, cap duration at 30 minutes, use async communication (Slack, email), and ensure every attendee has a clear role.

faq.q: Should we replace meetings with asynchronous communication?
faq.a: Not all meetings should be eliminated. Real-time collaboration is valuable for brainstorming, decision-making, and relationship building. Use async for updates and non-urgent discussion.

faq.q: Are daily standups worth the cost?
faq.a: Yes—15 minutes daily for alignment often delivers more value than a 1-hour weekly meeting, and it prevents longer, unplanned discussions.

faq.q: How often should we hold retrospectives?
faq.a: Monthly or bi-weekly retrospectives are ideal. Schedule them in advance so teams can plan discussion topics.

faq.q: What percentage of work time should be meetings?
faq.a: Best practice suggests keeping meetings to 10–15% of total work time. If meetings exceed 25–30% of time, your organization likely has a meeting efficiency problem.

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula—no external data required for updates
